summary:
The Fourth Dynasty of ancient Egypt, dating approximately 2613–2494 BC, represents the pinnacle of the Old Kingdom and is often called Egypt's pyramid age. The three pharaohs most closely associated with the Giza plateau — Khufu (Cheops), Khafre, and Menkaure — all reigned during this dynasty, and their monuments constitute the most celebrated ensemble of ancient structures in the world. Under Fourth Dynasty rule, the Egyptian state reached unprecedented levels of centralisation and architectural ambition.

Sneferu, the dynasty's founder, was an extraordinarily prolific builder who constructed at least three major pyramids at Meidum and Dahshur, including [[event:3735|the Bent Pyramid]] and [[event:3736|the Red Pyramid]]. These projects represented an experimental phase in pyramid design that ultimately produced the true pyramid form inherited by his son Khufu. Sneferu's reign also saw military campaigns into Nubia and Libya and extensive trade contacts with the Levantine coast, importing timber from Byblos.

Khufu's construction of [[event:1708|the Great Pyramid of Giza]] around 2580–2560 BC required a level of state organisation and resource mobilisation that has no parallel in the early ancient world. His son Khafre added the second pyramid and the [[event:1710|Great Sphinx]], while Menkaure built the third and smallest of the three main Giza pyramids. Together these monuments functioned as royal mortuary complexes, each with temples, causeways, and subsidiary pyramids forming a sacred landscape of extraordinary scale.

The administrative apparatus of the Fourth Dynasty was tightly controlled by the royal family. High offices were held almost exclusively by princes and close relatives of the pharaoh, creating a highly centralised government in which religious authority and political power were effectively fused. This concentration of power appears to have generated internal tensions that contributed to the more dispersed authority visible in the Fifth Dynasty. Nevertheless, the Fourth Dynasty's architectural legacy profoundly shaped Egyptian culture for millennia.
